stem: electronic form, may be reproduced or transmitted in any form or by any means, electronic or mechanical, including photocopying, recording, or by any information storage and retrieval system, without permission of the publisher. For information, write: Communications, Law School Admission Council, 662 Penn Street, Box 40, Newtown, PA, 18940- 0040. materials or services, and inclusion of licensed LSAT questions within this work does not imply the review or endorsement of LSAC. 1. After a major toll highway introduced a system of electronic toll paying, delays at all of its interchanges declined significantly. Travel time per car trip decreased by an average of 10 percent. Tailpipe pollution for each trip decreased commensurately. Despite this, the total air pollution from vehicles on that highway did not decrease measurably. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy in the information above?
choice_a: The highway began charging higher tolls when it switched to electronic toll paying.
choice_b: Even after the switch to electronic toll paying, there were sometimes long delays at the highway’s interchanges.
choice_c: The prospect of faster, more convenient travel induced more drivers to use the highway.
choice_d: Travel time on the highway for car trips under 30 kilometers (18.6 miles) did not decrease appreciably.
choice_e: Some drivers did not switch to the electronic system but instead continued to use cash to pay their tolls at toll booths.

stem: A lack of trust in one’s neighbors leads to their lack of respect for the law. A new study provides compelling evidence for this. Neighborhoods in which people routinely lock their doors have higher burglary rates than neighborhoods in which people do not routinely lock their doors. The reasoning in the argument is flawed in that the argument
choice_a: treats something that is merely sufficient to produce a result as if it were necessary to produce that result
choice_b: draws a moral conclusion from evidence that could only support a factual conclusion
choice_c: bases its conclusion on data that are contradictory
choice_d: asserts in a premise what it is trying to establish in its conclusion
choice_e: treats what could be the effect of something as if it were the cause of that thing

stem: In recent decades, government efforts to fight counterfeiting have been extremely successful, especially efforts to remove counterfeit bills from circulation. Yet counterfeiters are not finding it at all difficult to get away with passing counterfeit bills to merchants and even banks. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy in the information above?
choice_a: Government information campaigns that teach merchants and bank tellers how to detect counterfeit bills are more effective than ever.
choice_b: Governments are continually developing new currency designs with features that are difficult for criminals to counterfeit.
choice_c: Counterfeiters are generally unaware that the percentage of fake bills in circulation is the lowest it has ever been.
choice_d: Government success in removing counterfeit bills from circulation has caused merchants and bank tellers to become lax in checking for counterfeit bills.
choice_e: Governments are spending larger and larger sums of money in their efforts to remove counterfeit bills from circulation.

stem: If a civilization as technologically advanced as human civilization existed on another planet and that planet were within 50 light years of Earth, that civilization would have found evidence of intelligent life on Earth and could have easily contacted us. Scientists can thus rule out the possibility of finding a civilization as technologically advanced as our own within 50 light years of Earth. Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?
choice_a: Scientists who are searching for evidence of extraterrestrial life forms generally focus their search on evidence of technologically advanced life forms.
choice_b: There is no reason to doubt the possibility that there are technologically advanced civilizations on planets more than 50 light years from Earth.
choice_c: If scientists received a message from a technologically advanced civilization on another planet, they would be able to decipher it fully.
choice_d: A technologically advanced civilization on another planet would want to communicate with intelligent life that it detected on Earth.
choice_e: Intelligent life forms on other planets would be able to recognize all signs of intelligent life on Earth.

stem: Recently, many traffic lights and street markings were temporarily removed from a heavily traveled street in a major metropolitan area. Given that this street experiences significant volumes of automobile traffic, the number of accidents on the street was expected to increase. However, even though the street experienced no reduction in traffic, the number of accidents was greatly reduced. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent conflict described above?
choice_a: People often disregard traffic lights and street markings.
choice_b: The lack of traffic lights and street markings caused drivers to drive more cautiously.
choice_c: Most drivers were not aware that traffic lights and street markings had been removed.
choice_d: Traffic lights and street markings are intended to have benefits in addition to those related to safety.
choice_e: Drivers were given advance notice that the traffic lights and street markings would be removed.

stem: Some have argued that body size influences mating decisions throughout all societies. Their argument rests largely on self-reports of university-age students and on analyses of personal advertisements in newspapers for dating partners. The reasoning in the argument described above is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that the argument
choice_a: concludes that one kind of event causes another kind of event without ruling out the possibility that both kinds of events are the result of a third kind of event
choice_b: bases a conclusion on a sample that may be unrepresentative of the population about which the conclusion is drawn
choice_c: concludes that an effect has only one cause in the face of evidence that the effect has multiple causes
choice_d: uses a claim that applies only to entire societies to draw a conclusion about individual persons
choice_e: draws a universal conclusion on the basis of a very small number of individual cases

stem: Journalist: The new mayor is undeniably bold. His assertions are made with utter certainty and confidence. While these kinds of assertions may make him popular with the public, they also demonstrate that he is not an introspective person. Which one of the following is an assumption required by the journalist’s argument?
choice_a: Introspective people do not make assertions with utter certainty and confidence.
choice_b: Politicians who make assertions with utter certainty and confidence are popular with the public.
choice_c: People who are bold make public assertions with utter certainty and confidence.
choice_d: People whose assertions are uncertain and lack confidence are introspective.
choice_e: Politicians who are not bold are unpopular with the public.

stem: While studying a large colony of macaque monkeys, scientists interacting with baby monkeys under a week old found that the babies would imitate some, but not all, of the scientists’ actions. The babies readily smacked their lips and stuck out their tongues when the scientists did, but stared impassively when the scientists opened and closed their mouths or made hand gestures. Of these four kinds of actions, only lip smacking and sticking out the tongue are used by adult macaques when interacting with babies. The statements above, if true, most strongly support which one of the following?
choice_a: Baby macaques under a week old are natural mimics of whatever they see.
choice_b: Baby macaques under a week old cannot imitate hand gestures because they do not yet control the necessary muscles.
choice_c: Adult macaques use lip smacking and sticking out the tongue to entertain infant macaques.
choice_d: Baby macaques under a week old mistake the scientists interacting with them for adult macaques.
choice_e: Baby macaques under a week old only imitate human gestures also used by adult macaques.

stem: Some scientists believe that small humanoid skeletons found on an Indonesian island are the remains of human beings with a growth disorder. It is more likely that they represent a distinct human species that became smaller over time due to environmental pressure. These skeletons do not fit the pattern of known growth disorders. And evidence suggests that certain fox and mouse species on the island have evolved into smaller versions of their common counterparts. Which one of the following most accurately expresses the conclusion drawn in the argument?
choice_a: Some scientists believe that the humanoid skeletons are the remains of human beings with a growth disorder.
choice_b: It is more likely that the humanoid skeletons represent a distinct human species than that they are the remains of human beings with a growth disorder.
choice_c: The humanoid skeletons do not fit the pattern of known growth disorders.
choice_d: Certain fox and mouse species on an Indonesian island have evolved into smaller versions of their common counterparts.
choice_e: Environmental pressure can cause species living on islands to become smaller over time.

stem: The more sunlight our planet reflects back into space, the cooler the global atmosphere tends to become. Snow and ice reflect much more sunlight back into space than do ocean water or land without snow cover. Therefore, the greater the area of Earth’s surface that is covered with snow and ice, the cooler, on average, the global atmosphere is likely to become. Which one of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument?
choice_a: Low atmospheric temperatures are required for the formation of clouds that result in snow.
choice_b: Other factors besides the reflectivity of ice and snow affect the cooling of Earth’s atmosphere.
choice_c: Ocean water and land heated by sunlight in turn warm Earth’s atmosphere.
choice_d: The atmosphere derives most of its heat from the passage of sunlight through it.
choice_e: Lighter-colored soil reflects more sunlight back into space than does darker-colored soil.

stem: Nick: The Pincus family and their construction company have supported our university financially for decades. The university should not give the contract for building its new library to the family’s main competitor. Doing so would be disloyal to a friend of the university. Pedro: Accepting a donation does not oblige the university to give the donor any special privileges. If it did, then it wouldn’t really be a charitable contribution. We should award the contract to whatever company makes the most competitive bid. The dialogue provides the most support for the claim that Nick and Pedro disagree over whether
choice_a: loyalty should sometimes be a consideration in making business decisions
choice_b: the Pincus family and their construction company donated money for the purpose of acquiring special privileges from the university
choice_c: the acceptance of donations places a university under a special obligation to the donor
choice_d: the university should be more grateful to donors with a long history of financial support than to new donors
choice_e: the Pincus family’s construction company did not make the most competitive bid

stem: Ampicillin and other modern antibiotics kill a much wider variety of bacteria than penicillin does. They also carry higher profit margins, so drug companies now have an incentive to stop manufacturing the older, less profitable antibiotics. This could cause a penicillin shortage, forcing doctors to use the much more powerful new antibiotics in cases where they might otherwise be unnecessary. Thus, these newer antibiotics are likely to result in an outbreak of diseases caused by drug-resistant bacteria, since _______. The conclusion of the argument is most strongly supported if which one of the following completes the passage?
choice_a: drug-resistant bacteria flourish in the absence of competition from a wide variety of other bacteria
choice_b: older antibiotics like penicillin have been widely used for many decades
choice_c: a shortage of penicillin would drive up its price and profit margin
choice_d: treatment of diseases with the powerful new antibiotics is much more expensive than treatment with the older ones
choice_e: most bacteria that are resistant to penicillin are not resistant to ampicillin and other modern antibiotics

stem: Weingarten claims that keeping animals in zoos is unethical. He points out that it involves placing animals in unnatural environments merely for the sake of human amusement. However, since Weingarten sees nothing wrong with owning pets, and keeping pets surely involves placing an animal in an unnatural environment merely for human amusement, his claim should be rejected. The reasoning in the argument is flawed in that the argument
choice_a: takes for granted that Weingarten owns one or more pets
choice_b: inappropriately generalizes from a particular case
choice_c: misrepresents the conclusion of the opposing argument
choice_d: takes a necessary condition for a practice’s being unethical as a sufficient condition for its being so
choice_e: rejects a claim merely on the grounds that its proponent holds another view inconsistent with it

stem: Activist: President Zagel should resign, because she is unable to govern effectively given the widespread belief that she rigged the election. President Zagel: Over the last decade, scandals have forced two presidents of this country to resign. If I were to resign, the rest of the world would see us as a country whose political system is hopelessly unstable. That would be unacceptable, so I must remain in office. Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the activist’s argument in the face of President Zagel’s argument?
choice_a: A country whose election procedures are resistant to illegitimate manipulation will eventually become politically stable.
choice_b: The leader of a country should resign if doing so is likely to improve that country’s international reputation for political stability.
choice_c: If a president is involved in a scandal that is more serious than scandals that have forced previous leaders to resign, then that president should resign.
choice_d: If it can be conclusively proven that an officeholder rigged an election, then that officeholder should be removed from office.
choice_e: It is more important for a country to have a leader who can govern effectively than it is to be viewed by other countries as having a stable political system.

stem: A popular book argues that people who are successful in business have, without exception, benefited from a lot of luck on their way to success. But this is ridiculous. Anyone who has studied successful people knows that success requires a lot of hard work. The argument commits which one of the following errors of reasoning?
choice_a: It mistakes the claim that something is required for a purpose for the claim that it is sufficient for that purpose.
choice_b: It accepts a view as authoritative without establishing the authority of the source of the view.
choice_c: It takes for granted in a premise what it is trying to prove in its conclusion.
choice_d: It treats an effect of something as the cause of that thing.
choice_e: It attacks the source of an argument rather than attacking the substance of that argument.

stem: University president: When a faculty member’s falsification of research was uncovered, the media treated it as evidence of the university’s low standards, even though in truth it was a mere case of dishonesty. But since vigilance with respect to academic standards is always necessary, it’s good that standards have become a topic of discussion. Which one of the following conforms most closely to the principle illustrated above?
choice_a: The latest government scandal was caused primarily by a lack of oversight, which in turn led to corruption. Since no amount of oversight can eliminate all corruption, it is important that the problems with oversight are not the only topic of discussion.
choice_b: The latest government scandal has been attributed to lack of oversight, although the true cause of the scandal was simple corruption. Nonetheless, this discussion of oversight is welcome, because oversight is important in its own right.
choice_c: The latest government scandal has been attributed to both lack of oversight and corruption. As a result, these important concerns are now being discussed. So, despite the harm that it caused, it is good that the scandal occurred.
choice_d: The latest government scandal has been analyzed as a case of simple corruption, although corruption had little to do with it. Because the true cause of the scandal was lack of oversight, attributing the cause of the scandal to simple corruption is harmful.
choice_e: The latest government scandal has been analyzed as a case of simple corruption, with no mention of the role played by lack of oversight. Nonetheless, the focus on corruption is welcome, because corruption played the largest role in the scandal.

stem: Politician: Over the next decade, our city will be replacing all of its street signs with signs that are designed for improved readability. But since no one is complaining about the current signs, installing the new ones is a colossal waste of time and money. Which one of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the politician’s argument?
choice_a: What features of the new street signs improve the readability of the signs?
choice_b: Are the new street signs considerably more expensive to manufacture than the current street signs were?
choice_c: What percentage of its street signs does the city replace annually in the course of ordinary maintenance?
choice_d: Do any other cities plan to replace their street signs with signs designed for improved readability?
choice_e: Were experts consulted when the new street signs were designed?

stem: A large survey of scientists found that almost all accept Wang’s Law, and almost all know the results of the Brown-Eisler Experiment. But those results together with Wang’s Law contradict the Minsk Hypothesis. Therefore, most of the scientists surveyed reject the Minsk Hypothesis. The argument requires assuming which one of the following?
choice_a: The scientists surveyed are generally aware that the results of the Brown-Eisler Experiment together with Wang’s Law contradict the Minsk Hypothesis.
choice_b: The scientists in the survey who know the results of the Brown-Eisler Experiment are exactly the same ones who accept Wang’s Law.
choice_c: Almost all of the scientists surveyed are familiar with the way in which the results of the Brown-Eisler Experiment were obtained.
choice_d: The sample is large enough to be representative of scientists in the field.
choice_e: Wang’s Law has in fact been shown to be true.

stem: Any literary translation is a compromise between two goals that cannot be entirely reconciled: faithfulness to the meaning of the text and faithfulness to the original author’s style. Thus, even the most skillful translation will be at best a flawed approximation of the original work. Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the reasoning in the argument above?
choice_a: A translation of a literary work should be entirely faithful to neither the meaning of the text nor the original author’s style.
choice_b: If a literary translation is flawed as an approximation of the original work, it cannot be regarded as a successful compromise between faithfulness to the meaning of the text and faithfulness to the original author’s style.
choice_c: The most skillful literary translation of a work will not necessarily be the most balanced compromise between faithfulness to the meaning of the text and faithfulness to the original author’s style.
choice_d: Any translation that is not entirely faithful to both the meaning of the text and the original author’s style will be at best a flawed approximation of that work.
choice_e: Not even the most skillful literary translation could be faithful to both the literal meaning of the text and the original author’s style.

stem: Sociologist: Television, telephones, and other electronic media encourage imprecise, uncritical thinking. Yet critical thinking is the only adequate protection against political demagogues, who seek to exploit people by presenting emotionally loaded language as an objective description of reality. If the sociologist’s statements are true, then each of the following statements could be true EXCEPT:
choice_a: There are no political demagogues in some highly technological societies.
choice_b: Political demagogues are not the only ones who seek to exploit people by presenting emotionally loaded language as an objective description of reality.
choice_c: Highly emotional people are more easily exploited than less emotional people.
choice_d: The mere presence of an orderly system of government in a society provides adequate protection against political demagogues.
choice_e: The mere presence of electronic communications technology in a society provides adequate protection against the erosion of media freedoms.

stem: People with higher-than-average blood levels of a normal dietary by-product called homocysteine are twice as likely to be diagnosed with Alzheimer’s disease as are those with average or below-average homocysteine levels. Thus, it is likely that the risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease could be reduced by including in one’s diet large amounts of B vitamins and folic acid, which convert homocysteine into substances known to have no relation to Alzheimer’s disease. Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?
choice_a: Many Alzheimer’s patients have normal homocysteine levels.
choice_b: The substances into which homocysteine is converted can sometimes have harmful effects unrelated to Alzheimer’s disease.
choice_c: B vitamins and folic acid are not metabolized by the body very efficiently when taken in the form of vitamin-mineral supplements.
choice_d: People whose relatives contracted Alzheimer’s disease are much more likely to develop Alzheimer’s than those whose relatives did not.
choice_e: Alzheimer’s disease tends to increase the levels of homocysteine in the blood.

stem: Consumer advocate: Economists reason that price gouging—increasing the price of goods when no alternative seller is available—is efficient because it allocates goods to people whose willingness to pay more shows that they really need those goods. But willingness to pay is not proportional to need. In the real world, some people simply cannot pay as much as others. As a result, a price increase will allocate goods to the people with the most money, not to those with the most need. Which one of the following most accurately describes the role played in the consumer advocate’s argument by the claim that willingness to pay is not proportional to need?
choice_a: It disputes one explanation in order to make way for an alternative explanation.
choice_b: It is the overall conclusion of the argument.
choice_c: It is a component of reasoning disputed in the argument.
choice_d: It is a general principle whose validity the argument questions.
choice_e: It denies a claim that the argument takes to be assumed in the reasoning that it rejects.

stem: Zoologist: Plants preferentially absorb heavy nitrogen from rainwater. Heavy nitrogen consequently becomes concentrated in the tissues of herbivores, and animals that eat meat in turn exhibit even higher concentrations of heavy nitrogen in their bodily tissues. We compared bone samples from European cave bears of the Ice Age with blood samples from present-day bears fed meat-enriched diets, and the levels of heavy nitrogen present in these samples were identical. Thus, the prehistoric European cave bears were not exclusively herbivores. Which one of the following, if true, would most strengthen the zoologist’s argument?
choice_a: Plants can also absorb heavy nitrogen from a variety of sources other than rainwater.
choice_b: The rate at which heavy nitrogen accumulated in the blood of Ice Age herbivores can be inferred from samples of their bones.
choice_c: The same number of samples was taken from present-day bears as was taken from Ice Age cave bears.
choice_d: Bone samples from present-day bears fed meat-enriched diets exhibit the same levels of heavy nitrogen as do their blood samples.
choice_e: The level of heavy nitrogen in the bones of any bear fed a meat-enriched diet is the same as that in the bones of any other meat-eating bear.

stem: Biologist: Some computer scientists imagine that all that is required for making an artificial intelligence is to create a computer program that encapsulates the information contained in the human genome. They are mistaken. The operation of the human brain is governed by the interactions of proteins whose structures are encoded in the human genome. Which one of the following is an assumption required by the biologist’s argument?
choice_a: The functions of the human brain are governed by processes that cannot be simulated by a computer.
choice_b: The interactions of the proteins that govern the operation of the human brain are not determined by the information contained in the human genome.
choice_c: The only way to create an artificial intelligence is to model it on the operation of the human brain.
choice_d: The amount of information contained in the human genome is too large to be easily encapsulated by a computer program.
choice_e: It is much more difficult to write a program that encapsulates the interactions of proteins than to write a program that encapsulates the information contained in the human genome.

stem: Some advertisers offer certain consumers home computers free of charge. Advertisements play continuously on the computers’ screens whenever they are in use. As consumers use the computers to browse the Internet, information about their browsing patterns is sent to the advertisers, enabling them to transmit to each consumer advertising that accurately reflects his or her individual interests. The advertisers can afford to offer the computers for free because of the increased sales that result from this precise targeting of individual consumers. Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?
choice_a: At least some consumers who use a computer offered free of charge by advertisers for browsing the Internet spend more money on purchases from those advertisers than they would if they did not use such a computer to browse the Internet.
choice_b: No advertisers could offer promotions that give away computers free of charge if consumers never used those computers to browse the Internet.
choice_c: There are at least some consumers who browse the Internet using computers offered free of charge by the advertisers and who, if they did not use those computers to browse the Internet, would spend little if any money on purchases from those advertisers.
choice_d: The advertisers would not be able to offer the computers absolutely free of charge if advertisements that accurately reflected the interests of the computers’ users did not play continuously across the computers’ screens whenever they were in use.
choice_e: Consumers who use a computer offered free of charge by the advertisers can sometimes choose to abstain from having information about their browsing patterns sent to the advertisers.

stem: Some eloquent speakers impress their audiences with the vividness and clarity of the messages conveyed. Speakers who resort to obscenity, however, are not genuinely eloquent, so none of these speakers impress their audiences. The flawed reasoning in which one of the following is most similar to that in the argument above?
choice_a: A culture without myths will also lack fundamental moral certainties. Thus, this culture must lack fundamental moral certainties, since it is devoid of myth.
choice_b: There are authors who write one page a day and produce one book per year. Serious authors, however, do not write one page per day, so some authors who write one book a year are not serious.
choice_c: Cities that are centers of commerce are always centers of industry as well. It follows that some centers of commerce are small cities, since there are centers of industry that are not small cities.
choice_d: Most farmers like living in rural areas. Since Carla is not a farmer, she probably would not enjoy living in the country.
choice_e: Sculptors sometimes produce significant works of art. But musicians are not sculptors. Hence, musicians never produce significant works of art.

stem: Both passages seek an answer to which one of the following questions?
choice_a: Why are explanations in the field of exercise physiology so inconclusive?
choice_b: What is the best way for bodybuilders to begin training again after a period of inactivity?
choice_c: Why is building muscle easier for people who have done so in the past?
choice_d: Is muscle memory a purely psychological phenomenon?
choice_e: Is there a psychological basis for the increases in muscle size and strength that result from exercise?

stem: Passage B, but not passage A, seeks to achieve its purpose by
choice_a: questioning the reality of an alleged phenomenon
choice_b: discussing the results of a recent scientific experiment
choice_c: appealing to the reader’s personal experience
choice_d: considering the psychological factors involved in bodybuilding
choice_e: speculating about the cause of an observed phenomenon

stem: Passage B, unlike passage A, suggests that the phenomenon of muscle memory might be due to
choice_a: muscle cells’ ability to merge with stem cells
choice_b: the body’s ability to adapt to consistent training
choice_c: psychological factors
choice_d: a cell death program known as apoptosis
choice_e: the neurons that stimulate muscles

stem: It can be inferred from the passages that the author of passage A
choice_a: is more certain than the author of passage B about the existence of muscle memory
choice_b: probably agrees with the author of passage B about the explanation for muscle memory
choice_c: was probably not aware of the scientific research that is described in passage B
choice_d: probably disagrees with the author of passage B about how muscle cells’ nuclei affect muscle strength
choice_e: tends to be more skeptical than the author of passage B about conclusions drawn about one species on the basis of experiments involving another species

stem: Given the style and tone of each passage, which one of the following is most likely to correctly describe the expected audience of each passage?
choice_a: Passage A: skeptics of the phenomenon under discussion Passage B: people with personal experience of the phenomenon under discussion
choice_b: Passage A: scientific researchers Passage B: athletic trainers and coaches
choice_c: Passage A: athletes who work with a trainer Passage B: people who pursue a fitness program on their own
choice_d: Passage A: bodybuilders Passage B: a general audience
choice_e: Passage A: sports psychologists Passage B: exercise physiologists

stem: The author of passage B would be most likely to hold which one of the following views about the characterization of muscle memory offered in the first sentence of passage A?
choice_a: It confirms that bodybuilders’ experiences should not be accepted at face value.
choice_b: It reflects a dichotomy between athletes’ experience and processes occurring at the cellular level of their muscles.
choice_c: It would not be accepted by most athletes who have started retraining after a period of inactivity.
choice_d: It is less apt now in light of recent research than it was before that research was conducted.
choice_e: It stems from a fundamental misunderstanding of the principles of exercise psychology.

stem: Which one of the following is explicitly mentioned in passage B but not in passage A?

stem: Cool weather typically weakens muscle power in cold-blooded creatures. In the veiled chameleon, a cold-blooded animal, the speed at which the animal can retract its tongue declines dramatically as the temperature falls. However, the speed at which this chameleon can extend its tongue does not decline much as the temperature falls. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy in the information above?
choice_a: Most cold-blooded animals are much more active in warmer weather than in cooler weather.
choice_b: Many cold-blooded animals, including the veiled chameleon, have tongues that can extend quite a distance.
choice_c: Veiled chameleons are found in a wide range of habitats, including ones with wide variations in temperature and ones with moderate climates.
choice_d: In the veiled chameleon, tongue retraction is powered by muscles, whereas tongue extension is driven by energy stored in a rubber band-like sheath.
choice_e: Compared with the muscles in the tongues of most cold-blooded animals, the retraction muscles in the veiled chameleon’s tongue are considerably stronger.

choice_a: Acme’s earnings for last year, when accurately stated, are below $1 million

stem: Hospital patients generally have lower infection rates and require shorter hospital stays if they are housed in private rooms rather than semiprivate rooms. Yet in Woodville’s hospital, which has only semiprivate rooms, infection rates and length of stays are typically the same as in several nearby hospitals where most of the rooms are private, even though patients served by these hospitals are very similar to those served by Woodville’s hospital. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent conflict in the information above?
choice_a: Many of the doctors who routinely treat patients in Woodville’s hospital also routinely treat patients in one or more of the nearby hospitals.
choice_b: Most of the nearby hospitals were built within the last 10 years, whereas Woodville’s hospital was built about 50 years ago.
choice_c: Infection is more likely to be spread where people come into close contact with one another than where they do not.
choice_d: Woodville’s hospital has a policy of housing one patient per room in semiprivate rooms whenever possible.
choice_e: Woodville’s hospital is located in its central business district, whereas most of the nearby hospitals are located outside their municipalities’ business districts.

stem: Economist: Unemployment will soon decrease. If total government spending significantly increases next year, the economy will be stimulated in the short term and unemployment will decrease. If, on the other hand, total government spending significantly decreases next year, businesses will retain more of their earnings in the short term and employ more workers, thereby decreasing unemployment. The conclusion drawn by the economist is properly inferred if which one of the following is assumed?
choice_a: Either total government spending will significantly decrease next year or else total government spending will significantly increase next year.
choice_b: Government officials are currently implementing policies that are intended to reduce unemployment.
choice_c: If there is a significantly increased demand for workers, then there will be a significant decrease in unemployment.
choice_d: A significant increase in total government spending will slow the economy in the long run.
choice_e: If the economy is not stimulated and businesses do not retain more of their earnings, then unemployment will not decrease.

stem: Scientist: Laboratory animals have access to ample food, and they get relatively little exercise. These factors can skew the results of research using animals, since such studies often rely on the assumption that the animal subjects are healthy. For instance, animal studies that purport to show that extreme caloric restriction can extend lifespans take for granted that their subjects were not overfed to begin with. The scientist’s argument requires assuming which one of the following?
choice_a: Laboratory animals are healthy if they are fed a carefully restricted diet and get plenty of exercise.
choice_b: Laboratory conditions that provide animals with ample food but relatively little exercise can be unhealthy for the animals.
choice_c: It is not unusual for animals outside of laboratory settings to have access to ample food and get relatively little exercise.
choice_d: Some animal studies take into consideration the differences between the living conditions of laboratory animals and those of other animals.
choice_e: When provided with unlimited food over a long period of time, animals show little day-to-day variation in their eating habits.

stem: Trade negotiator: Increasing economic prosperity in a country tends to bring political freedom to its inhabitants. Therefore, it is wrong for any country to adopt trade policies that are likely to seriously hinder growth in the prosperity of any other country. Which one of the following principles, if valid, would most help to justify the trade negotiator’s reasoning?
choice_a: Every country should adopt at least some policies that encourage the development of political freedom in other countries.
choice_b: Both economic prosperity and political freedom can contribute to the overall well-being of any country’s inhabitants.
choice_c: The primary reason that any country seeks economic prosperity is to foster political freedom in that country.
choice_d: A country should not do anything that might hinder the growth of political freedom in any other country.
choice_e: It is wrong for any country to adopt trade policies that might diminish the prosperity of its inhabitants.

stem: Whenever an artist endowed with both a high level of artistic skill and a high degree of creativity combines these two abilities in the process of creating an artwork, the resulting product is a great work of art. Moreover, no work of art can be great unless both of these elements are combined in its execution. Thus, great works of art are necessarily rare. Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?
choice_a: Not every artist possesses a high level of artistic skill.
choice_b: A high degree of creativity and a high level of artistic skill are seldom combined in the creation of a work of art.
choice_c: An artist endowed with a high degree of creativity and a high level of artistic skill will necessarily produce great works of art.
choice_d: Few artists are endowed with a high degree of creativity.
choice_e: Anyone endowed with both a high level of artistic skill and a high degree of creativity will produce only a few great works of art.

stem: Cereal advertisement: Fitness experts say that regular exercise is the most effective way to become physically fit, and studies have shown that adults who eat cereal every day exercise more regularly than adults who do not eat cereal. So by eating Fantastic Flakes every morning, you too will be on the most effective path to physical fitness. The argumentation in the advertisement is flawed in that it
choice_a: infers a cause from a mere correlation
choice_b: presumes, without providing justification, that Fantastic Flakes are more nutritious than other cereals
choice_c: infers that a given factor is the sole predictor of a result merely on the grounds that the factor has been shown to contribute to that result
choice_d: draws a conclusion about all adults from a sample that is too small to be representative

stem: Journalist: Some critics argue that as the entertainment value of news reporting increases, the caliber of that reporting decreases. choice_b: The greatest journalists have been entertainers who report the news.
choice_c: Journalistic greatness involves producing news that is very valuable in some sense.
choice_d: Entertainment and news are not mutually exclusive categories.

stem: Linguist: Three of the four subfamilies of the so-called “Austronesian” languages are found only among indigenous peoples in Taiwan, whereas the fourth is found on islands over a huge area stretching from Madagascar to the eastern Pacific Ocean. Since these subfamilies all originated in the same language, which must have been originally spoken in a single geographic location, these facts suggest that Taiwan is the homeland where Austronesian languages have been spoken longest and, hence, that Austronesian-speaking peoples originated in Taiwan and later migrated to other islands. Which one of the following most accurately expresses the overall conclusion drawn in the linguist’s argument?
choice_a: The Austronesian family of languages has four subfamilies, three of which are found only among indigenous peoples in Taiwan.
choice_b: Wherever most subfamilies of the Austronesian family of languages have been spoken longest is probably the homeland where Austronesian languages originated.
choice_c: Taiwan is probably the homeland where Austronesian languages have been spoken longest.
choice_d: Austronesian-speaking peoples originated in the homeland where Austronesian languages have been spoken longest.
choice_e: Austronesian-speaking peoples probably originated in Taiwan and later migrated to other islands.

stem: West: Of our company’s three quality control inspectors, Haynes is clearly the worst. Of the appliances that were returned to us last year because of quality control defects, half were inspected by Haynes. Young: But Haynes inspects significantly more than half the appliances we sell each year. Young responds to West’s argument by
choice_a: contending that the argument presupposes what it is trying to prove
choice_b: questioning the relevance of West’s conclusion
choice_c: disputing the accuracy of one of the argument’s stated premises
choice_d: arguing for a less extreme version of West’s conclusion
choice_e: denying one of the argument’s presuppositions

stem: While playing a game with a ball, both Emma and John carelessly ignored the danger their game posed to nearby objects. An errant throw by John struck and broke a neighbor’s window. Because his throw broke the window, John, but not Emma, should be required to perform chores for the neighbor as compensation for the damage. Which one of the following conforms most closely to the principle illustrated above?
choice_a: While looking after her neighbor’s pets, Laura left the door to her neighbor’s house unlocked. Fortunately, nothing bad happened as a result. But her neighbor should not trust Laura to look after her pets in the future.
choice_b: Gerald hired Linda and Seung to move his furniture to a new residence. Linda and Seung carefully followed Gerald’s instructions, but not all of the furniture fit in the moving truck. Gerald should still be required to pay Linda and Seung for the work they did.
choice_c: Terry and Chris were racing their cars on a public street. Chris lost control of his car and struck a parked car. Chris, but not Terry, should be required to pay to repair the damage.
choice_d: Alexis and Juan rented a boat for the afternoon. Because of improper use by the previous renter, the boat’s engine malfunctioned during their excursion. The boat’s owner should be required to refund Alexis’s and Juan’s rental fees.
choice_e: Susan and Leland disregarded posted warnings in order to skate on a frozen pond. When the ice broke, Susan’s ankle was injured. Susan cannot hold the pond’s owners responsible for her injuries.

stem: Psychology researchers observed that parents feel emotion while singing to their infants. The researchers hypothesized that this emotion noticeably affects the sound of the singing. To test this hypothesis the parents were recorded while singing to their infants and while singing with no infant present. They were instructed to make the two renditions as similar as possible. These recordings were then played for psychologists who had not observed the recordings being made. For 80 percent of the recordings, these psychologists were able to correctly identify, by listening alone, which recordings were of parents singing to their children. The researchers concluded that their hypothesis was correct. Which one of the following, if true, would most strengthen the researchers’ reasoning?
choice_a: A separate study by the same researchers found that parents feel more emotion when singing to their own children than when singing to other children.
choice_b: Some, but not all, of the parents in the study realized that their song renditions were being recorded.
choice_c: Parents displayed little emotion when singing with no child or adult present.
choice_d: When a person feels emotion, that emotion provokes involuntary physiological responses that affect the vocal cords and lungs.
choice_e: Most of the parents who participated in the study believed that the emotion they felt while singing to their infants affected their singing.

stem: Many scholars claim that Shakespeare’s portrayal of Richard III was extremely inaccurate, arguing that he derived that portrayal from propagandists opposed to Richard III. But these claims are irrelevant for appreciating Shakespeare’s work. The character of Richard III as portrayed in Shakespeare’s drama is fascinating and illuminating both aesthetically and morally, regardless of its relation to historical fact. Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the reasoning in the argument above?
choice_a: In historical drama, the aesthetic value of the work is not necessarily undermined by historical inaccuracies.
choice_b: In dealing with real people, dramatists should reflect their lives accurately.
choice_c: Shakespeare’s historical importance puts him beyond the scope of all literary criticism.
choice_d: History is always told by propagandists from the winning side.
choice_e: Historical inaccuracies should be corrected only when they impugn the reputations of good people.

stem: Voter: Our prime minister is evidently seeking a job at an international organization. Anyone seeking a job at an international organization would surely spend a lot of time traveling abroad, and our prime minister has spent more days abroad than at home so far this year. Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the voter’s argument?
choice_a: Kao must be a golfer. Kao is planning to run for office, and most people who run for office play golf.
choice_b: Franklin will lose the coming election. The opposing candidate has better policy ideas and brings more relevant experience to the job.
choice_c: Ramirez is evidently able to control the traffic signals. Just now, as Ramirez approached the curb, the traffic signal changed from red to green.
choice_d: Thompson must be negotiating a personal loan. Thompson was at the bank yesterday, and people who are negotiating a personal loan go to the bank to meet with a loan agent.
choice_e: McKinsey must have committed a crime at some point. After all, despite extensive background checks no one has been able to show that McKinsey has never committed a crime.

stem: It is pointless to debate the truth of the law of noncontradiction, a fundamental logical principle according to which two statements that contradict each other cannot both be true. For a debate to be productive, participants must hold some basic principles in common. But the principles held in common in a debate over the law of noncontradiction would be much less certain than that law, so it matters little whether the law of noncontradiction can be defended on the basis of those principles. Which one of the following most accurately expresses the overall conclusion drawn in the argument?
choice_a: It is pointless to debate the truth of the law of noncontradiction.
choice_b: Statements that contradict each other cannot both be true.
choice_c: The participants in a productive debate must hold at least some basic principles in common.
choice_d: The law of noncontradiction is a principle that the participants in a productive debate must hold in common.
choice_e: Any principles that could be used to defend the law of noncontradiction are less certain than it is.

stem: Pundit: For many high school graduates, attending a university would be of no help in getting a corporate job. The attributes corporations value most in potential employees are initiative, flexibility, and the ability to solve practical problems. Many new high school graduates have these attributes already. The pundit’s argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
choice_a: fails to establish that university graduates do not have initiative, flexibility, and the ability to solve practical problems
choice_b: overlooks the possibility that corporations may require an attribute that potential employees can obtain only by attending a university
choice_c: provides no justification for the presumption that corporations only hire employees who have initiative, flexibility, and the ability to solve practical problems
choice_d: takes for granted that the only reason that high school graduates go on to attend university is to improve their job prospects
choice_e: takes for granted that initiative, flexibility, and the ability to solve practical problems are attributes that can be acquired through study

stem: Archaeologist: Neanderthals, a human-like species living 60,000 years ago, probably preserved meat by smoking it. Burnt lichen and grass have been found in many Neanderthal fireplaces. A fire of lichen and grass produces a lot of smoke but does not produce nearly as much heat or light as a wood fire. Which one of the following, if true, would most weaken the archaeologist’s argument?
choice_a: In close proximity to the fireplaces with lichen and grass are other fireplaces that, evidence suggests, burned material that produced more heat than smoke.
choice_b: In the region containing the Neanderthal fireplaces in which lichen and grass were burnt, no plants that could be burned more effectively to produce heat or light were available 60,000 years ago.
choice_c: Some of the fireplaces containing burnt lichen are in regions in which lichen is not believed to have been plentiful and so would have had to have been brought in from some distance.
choice_d: There is clear evidence that at least some groups of Neanderthals living more recently than 60,000 years ago developed methods of preserving meat other than smoking it.
choice_e: The ability to preserve meat through smoking would have made the Neanderthal humans less vulnerable to poor periods of hunting.

stem: Edgar: Some of the pumps supplying water to our region have been ordered shut down in order to protect a species of small fish. But it is absurd to inconvenience thousands of people for the sake of something so inconsequential. Rafaela: You’re missing the point. The threat to that fish species is a sign of a very serious threat to our water supply. The dialogue provides the most support for the claim that Edgar and Rafaela disagree over whether
choice_a: shutting down the pumps will actually inconvenience a large number of people
choice_b: the survival of the fish species is the only reason for shutting down the pumps
choice_c: species of small fish are inconsequential
choice_d: the order to shut down the pumps was legal
choice_e: shutting down the pumps will be sufficient to protect the fish species

stem: Only engineering is capable of analyzing the nature of a machine in terms of the successful working of the whole; physics and chemistry determine the material conditions necessary for this success, but cannot express the notion of purpose. Similarly, only physiology can analyze the nature of an organism in terms of organs’ roles in the body’s healthy functioning. Physics and chemistry cannot ascertain by themselves any of these operational principles. Which one of the following is an assumption required by the analogy?
choice_a: The functioning of the human organism is machine-like in nature.
choice_b: Physics and chemistry determine the material conditions required for good physiological functioning.
choice_c: The notion of purpose used by engineers to judge the success of machinery has an analog in organisms.
choice_d: Physiology as a science is largely independent of physics and chemistry.
choice_e: Biological processes are irreducible to mechanical or chemical processes.

stem: After a hepadnavirus inserts itself into a chromosome of an animal, fragments of the virus are passed on to all of that animal’s descendants. A hepadnavirus fragment is present in a chromosome of the zebra finch and in precisely the same location in a corresponding chromosome of the dark-eyed junco. The fact that these two bird species diverged from each other about 25 million years ago therefore means that the hepadnavirus is at least 25 million years old. Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
choice_a: Viruses can affect the evolution of an organism and can thereby influence the likelihood of their diverging into two species.
choice_b: The chromosomes of the zebra finch and the dark-eyed junco contain fragments of no virus other than the hepadnavirus.
choice_c: When a virus inserts itself into an animal’s chromosome, the insertion occurs at a random spot.
choice_d: Many bird species other than the zebra finch and the dark-eyed junco contain fragments of the hepadnavirus.
choice_e: The presence of a hepadnavirus in an animal species does not affect the likelihood of that species’ survival.

stem: The diet of Heliothis subflexa caterpillars consists entirely of fruit from plants of the genus Physalis. These fruit do not contain linolenic acid, which is necessary to the growth and maturation of many insects other than H. subflexa. Linolenic acid in an insect’s diet is also necessary for the production of a chemical called volicitin. While most caterpillar species have volicitin in their saliva, H. subflexa does not. Which one of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
choice_a: H. subflexa caterpillars synthesize linolenic acid within their bodies.
choice_b: Most species of caterpillar have sources of linolenic acid in their diets.
choice_c: Any caterpillar that has linolenic acid in its diet has volicitin in its saliva.
choice_d: A food source containing linolenic acid would be poisonous to H. subflexa caterpillars.
choice_e: No caterpillars other than H. subflexa eat fruit from plants of the genus Physalis.

stem: Politician: Democracy requires that there be no restrictions on the ability of citizens to share their ideas freely, without fear of reprisal. Therefore the right to have private conversations, unmonitored by the government, is essential to democracy. For a government to monitor conversations on the Internet would thus be a setback for democracy. Which one of the following most accurately describes the role played in the argument by the claim that democracy depends on the ability of citizens to share their ideas freely, without fear of reprisal?
choice_a: It is a claim for which no support is provided, and which is used to support only the argument’s main conclusion.
choice_b: It is a claim for which no support is provided, and which is used to support a claim that is itself used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
choice_c: It is a claim for which support is provided, and which is in turn used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
choice_d: It is the argument’s main conclusion and is inferred from two other statements in the argument, one of which is used to support the other.
choice_e: It is the argument’s main conclusion and is inferred from two other statements in the argument, neither of which is used to support the other.

stem: One way to compare chess-playing programs is to compare how they perform with fixed time limits per move. Given any two computers with which a chess-playing program is compatible, and given fixed time limits per move, such a program will have a better chance of winning on the faster computer. This is simply because the program will be able to examine more possible moves in the time allotted per move. Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?
choice_a: If one chess-playing program can examine more possible moves than a different chess-playing program run on the same computer under the same time constraints per move, the former program will have a better chance of winning than the latter.
choice_b: How fast a given computer is has no effect on which chess-playing computer programs can run on that computer.
choice_c: In general, the more moves a given chess-playing program is able to examine under given time constraints per move, the better the chances that program will win.
choice_d: If two different chess-playing programs are running on two different computers under the same time constraints per move, the program running on the faster computer will be able to examine more possible moves in the time allotted.
choice_e: If a chess-playing program is run on two different computers and is allotted more time to examine possible moves when running on the slow computer than when running on the fast computer, it will have an equal chance of winning on either computer.

stem: According to the passage, Rawls uses which one of the following devices to explain his theory?
choice_a: a thought experiment
choice_b: a process of elimination
choice_c: an empirical study of social institutions
choice_d: a deduction from a few basic principles
choice_e: a consideration of the meaning of words

stem: The purpose of the question in lines 6–8 is to
choice_a: point out an implausible feature of utilitarianism
choice_b: characterize utilitarianism as internally contradictory
choice_c: establish that utilitarianism must be true
choice_d: suggest the intuitive appeal of utilitarianism
choice_e: inquire into ways of supplementing utilitarianism

stem: The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to
choice_a: show why a once-dominant theory was abandoned
choice_b: describe the novel way in which a theory addresses a problem
choice_c: sketch the historical development of a celebrated theory
choice_d: debate the pros and cons of a complex theory
choice_e: argue for the truth of a controversial theory

stem: With which one of the following statements would both Rawls and the author of the passage be most likely to agree?
choice_a: There are situations in which it is permissible to treat the fulfillment of one person’s preferences as more important than the fulfillment of the majority’s preferences.
choice_b: Unless individuals set aside their own self-interest, they cannot make fair judgments about the distribution of goods.
choice_c: If an individual lacks a good, society must sometimes provide that good, even if this means taking it from others.
choice_d: Most people agree about which of the primary goods is the most valuable.
choice_e: It is fair to sacrifice the individual’s interests if doing so will maximize the satisfaction of the majority.

stem: The author’s stance toward Rawls’s theory is most accurately described as one of
choice_a: scholarly neutrality with respect both to its objectives and its development
choice_b: disdain for its pretensions camouflaged by declarations of respect for its author
choice_c: sympathy with its recommendations tempered with skepticism about its cogency
choice_d: enthusiasm for its aims mingled with doubts about its practicality
choice_e: admiration for its ingenuity coupled with misgivings about some of its implications

stem: Which one of the following would, if true, most call into question the claim in lines 49–51 of the passage?
choice_a: Most people value the fulfillment of their own preferences over the fulfillment of the preferences of strangers.
choice_b: It is impossible in practice for people to be ignorant of their stations in life, abilities, and tastes.
choice_c: Some people would be willing to risk a complete loss of one primary good for the chance of obtaining an enormous amount of another primary good.
choice_d: Few people believe that they would be satisfied with only a minimum amount of primary goods.
choice_e: People tend to overestimate the resources available for distribution and to underestimate their own needs.

stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main point of the passage?
choice_a: Approximately 40 percent of the African American population left the Southern U.S. between 1915 and 1960—an event historians refer to as the Great Migration.
choice_b: The Great Migration was triggered by an increased labor demand in the North due to the onset of World War I and a reduced labor demand in the South due to a boll weevil infestation.
choice_c: Because earlier migrants helped defray the financial costs of migration for later migrants, African American migration to the North accelerated at a time when income differences were narrowing.
choice_d: In migration movements, earlier migrants reduce the physical costs of moving and provide a cultural and linguistic cushion for later migrants.
choice_e: Although the Great Migration was initially triggered by the income differential between the North and South, other factors must be cited in order to explain its duration over several decades.

stem: According to the passage, the Great Migration did not start earlier than 1915 because
choice_a: the income gap between the North and South was not large enough to induce people to migrate
choice_b: the cost of living in the North was prohibitively high before World War I
choice_c: industrial jobs in the North required specialized training unavailable in the South
choice_d: previous migration had yet to develop sufficient momentum to induce further migration
choice_e: agricultural jobs in the South paid very well before the boll weevil infestation

stem: The third and fourth paragraphs of the passage function primarily to
choice_a: cast doubt upon a historical explanation presented in the first paragraph
choice_b: survey the repercussions of a historical event described in the first two paragraphs
choice_c: derive a historical model from evidence presented in the first two paragraphs
choice_d: answer a question raised in the second paragraph about a historical event
choice_e: provide additional evidence for historical claims made in the first paragraph

stem: The authors of the passage would be most likely to agree with which one of the following statements?
choice_a: Expected financial gains alone may not be a reliable indicator of the likelihood that an individual will migrate.
choice_b: A complete explanation of the Great Migration must begin with an account of what triggered nineteenth-century migrations to the North.
choice_c: The Great Migration is not parallel in its broadest patterns to most other known migration movements.
choice_d: Most large-scale migrations can be adequately explained in terms of the movement of people from lower- to higher-income regions.
choice_e: Large-scale migrations generally did not occur until the early twentieth century, when significant interregional income differences arose as a result of rapid industrialization.

stem: The primary purpose of the last sentence of the second paragraph is to
choice_a: indicate why previous research on the Great Migration has been misguided
choice_b: extend the authors’ explanation of the causes of the Great Migration to include later events
choice_c: challenge the traditional view that Northern wages were higher than Southern wages prior to 1915
choice_d: present a fact about the Great Migration that the authors seek to explain
choice_e: suggest that the Great Migration cannot be explained

stem: The passage provides the most support for which one of the following statements?
choice_a: The highest-paying agricultural jobs in the South prior to 1915 did not pay more than the lowest-paying manufacturing jobs in the North.
choice_b: The overall cost of migrating from the South to the North in the twentieth century was lower for the earliest migrants because there were more of the highest-paying jobs available for them to choose from.
choice_c: The North–South income gap increased around 1915 because of the increase in demand for labor in the North and the decrease in demand for labor in the South.
choice_d: The average wages in the South, though dramatically lower than the average wages in the North, held roughly steady for all workers during the 1910s and 1920s.
choice_e: Most migrants in the Great Migration made at least one trip back to the South to provide help and information to other people who were considering migrating as well.

stem: Which one of the following, if true, would provide the most support for the authors’ analysis of the Great Migration?
choice_a: The average amount of time it took new migrants to find employment in the North grew at a steady rate between 1915 and 1960.
choice_b: In general, communities of African Americans in the North consisted largely of individuals who shared a common geographic place of origin in the South.
choice_c: Housing prices in the North fluctuated between high and low extremes from 1915 to 1960, while housing prices in the South remained relatively constant.
choice_d: To maintain a steady rate of recruitment after World War I, Northern employers had to send more and more labor agents to recruit employees in the South.
choice_e: There was a large-scale reverse migration of African Americans back to Southern locations later in the twentieth century.

stem: Both passages are primarily concerned with answering which one of the following questions?
choice_a: How is insider trading defined?
choice_b: Should there be severer penalties for insider trading?
choice_c: Why do investors engage in insider trading?
choice_d: Is insider trading harmful to the stock market?
choice_e: What is the best means of regulating insider trading?

stem: In their attitudes toward stock trades based on inside information, the author of passage A and the author of passage B, respectively, may be most accurately described as
choice_a: positive and neutral
choice_b: positive and negative
choice_c: neutral and negative
choice_d: neutral and neutral
choice_e: negative and negative

stem: The authors would be most likely to agree that
choice_a: insider trading tends to undermine investor confidence in the stock market
choice_b: all information should be available to all market participants at the same time
choice_c: it is appropriate for investors to seek to gain an advantage by superior stock analysis
choice_d: insider nontrading should be regulated to the same extent as insider trading
choice_e: insider trading is the best means for disseminating information possessed by insiders

stem: Which one of the following laws would conform most closely to the position articulated by the author of passage A but not that articulated by the author of passage B?
choice_a: a law that prohibits trading based on information that is not shared by everyone
choice_b: a law that permits trading based on information gained from analysis of a stock but prohibits trading based on information obtained from one’s position within a company
choice_c: a law that prohibits trading that could reasonably be expected to undermine investors’ confidence in the stock market
choice_d: a law that legalizes selling based on inside information that a stock’s price ought to be dropping but prohibits buying based on inside information that it should be rising
choice_e: a law that legalizes trading based on inside information, as long as that information is not acquired by theft or other unlawful means

stem: Passage A, unlike passage B, seeks to advance its argument by
choice_a: applying general principles to particular examples
choice_b: pointing out similarities between a controversial activity and uncontroversial ones
choice_c: describing the consequences that would result from allowing an activity
choice_d: showing how a specific activity relates to a larger context
choice_e: examining the motivations of an activity’s participants

stem: The passages’ references to the analysis of information about stocks (lines 11–14, lines 40–42) are related in which one of the following ways?
choice_a: Passage A presents it as unnecessary, since all relevant information is already reflected in stock prices, whereas passage B presents it as necessary for making sound investment decisions.
choice_b: Passage A uses it as an example of an activity that compensates for the market’s lack of transparency, whereas passage B uses it as an example of an activity whose viability is conditional upon the transparency of the market.
choice_c: Passage A presents it as an activity that gives some investors an unfair advantage over others, whereas passage B presents it as an activity that increases the transparency of the market.
choice_d: Passage A presents it as comparable to the acquisition of inside information, whereas passage B contrasts it with the acquisition of inside information.
choice_e: Passage A treats it as an option available only to brokers and other stock-market professionals, whereas passage B treats it as an option available to ordinary investors as well.

stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the main point of the passage?
choice_a: In spite of troubling conceptual problems surrounding brain scan technology, its use in psychological research on mental activity has grown rapidly.
choice_b: The use of brain scans to depict mental activity relies on both a questionable premise and a misleading methodological approach.
choice_c: Contrary to what is usually asserted in the popular press, reason and emotion are probably not located in the prefrontal cortex and the amygdala, respectively.
choice_d: Although the fMRI is usually interpreted as a measure of metabolic activity in the brain, this interpretation is misguided and therefore leads to false results.
choice_e: The modular theory of mind has gained wide currency precisely because it is illustrated effectively by the images produced by the subtractive method.

stem: According to the modular theory of mind, as described in the passage, mental activity
choice_a: consists of distinct components in localized areas of the brain
choice_b: requires at least some metabolic activity in all parts of the brain
choice_c: involves physical processes over which people have only limited control
choice_d: is localized in the amygdala and the prefrontal cortex
choice_e: generally involves some sort of reason-giving

stem: The author of the passage would be most likely to agree with which one of the following statements regarding the subtractive method?
choice_a: Because the subtractive method masks distributed brain functions, empirical results derived using the method are invalid for medical applications.
choice_b: The subtractive method results in images that strongly support Uttal’s view that mental processes are simply properties of a more general mental activity.
choice_c: Brain scans of individuals experiencing anger that were produced using the subtractive method show that emotions are not actually seated in the amygdala.
choice_d: The subtractive method seems to strongly support the modular theory of mind because it creates an illusion that brain functions are localized.
choice_e: The view that the subtractive method depicts differential rates of oxygen use in the brain is based on a fundamental misconception of the method.

stem: A central function of the final paragraph of the passage is to
choice_a: criticize the research results described in the third paragraph on the grounds that they are incompatible with the basic premise described in the first paragraph
choice_b: suggest that the position articulated in the first paragraph needs to be modified to accommodate the results outlined in the third paragraph
choice_c: contend that the research method detailed in the third paragraph relies upon an outdated theoretical model described in the second paragraph
choice_d: argue that the empirical research outlined in the third paragraph points to the inadequacy of the competing views described in the first two paragraphs
choice_e: show why the type of empirical evidence discussed in the third paragraph does not defeat the argument presented in the second paragraph

stem: The author draws an analogy between brain scans and X-rays primarily in order to
choice_a: contrast a valid use of brain scans with one of more doubtful value
choice_b: suggest that new technology can influence the popularity of a scientific theory
choice_c: point to evidence that brain scans are less precise than other available technologies
choice_d: argue that X-ray images undermine a theory that brain scans are often used to support
choice_e: show how brain scan technology evolved from older technologies such as X-rays

stem: According to the passage, psychologist William Uttal contends that the various mental processes are likely to be
choice_a: independent modules that are based in different areas of the brain
choice_b: essentially an amalgamation of emotion and reason
choice_c: generally uniform in their rates of oxygen use
choice_d: detectable using brain scans enhanced by means of the subtractive method
choice_e: features of a general mental activity that is spread throughout the brain

stem: Which one of the following statements is most strongly supported by the passage?
choice_a: Although there are important exceptions, most cognition does in fact depend on independent modules located in specific regions of the brain.
choice_b: The modular theory of mind holds that regions of the brain that are not engaged in a specific cognitive task have a rate of oxygen use that is close to zero.
choice_c: During the performance of certain cognitive tasks, the areas of the brain that are most metabolically active show a rate of oxygen use that is higher than that of the rest of the brain.
choice_d: The baseline measurements of oxygen use taken for use in the subtractive method show that some regions of the brain have high metabolic activity at all times.
choice_e: When a brain scan subject experiences anger, the subtractive method shows several regions of the brain as “lit up” with metabolic activity.

stem: Which one of the following is most analogous to the manner in which fMRI scans of brain activity are typically interpreted, as described in the last two paragraphs?
choice_a: One particular district in the city voted for the new mayor by an unusually large margin, so the mayor could not have won without that district.
choice_b: A store launched a yearlong advertising campaign and had an increase in shoppers only during the summer, so the advertisements affected only the summer shoppers.
choice_c: Much more of the water supply is used by agricultural customers than by residential customers, so it is the agricultural sector that is impacted most severely when droughts occur.
choice_d: Internet traffic is highest during the evening hours, so most Internet traffic during these peak hours originates in homes rather than in office buildings.
choice_e: The cheetah is the world’s fastest land animal only for short distances, so most cheetahs cannot outrun another land animal over long distances.

stem: In a recent study of dust-mite allergy sufferers, one group slept on mite-proof bedding, while a control group slept on bedding that was not mite-proof. The group using mite-proof bedding had a 69 percent reduction in the dust-mite allergen in their mattresses, whereas there was no significant reduction in the control group. However, even though bedding is the main source of exposure to dust mites, no symptom reduction was reported in either group. Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent conflict in the statements above?
choice_a: Dust-mite allergens in bedding tend to irritate many allergy sufferers’ nasal passages more than do the same allergens in other locations, such as carpets.
choice_b: When people report their own allergy symptoms, they tend to exaggerate the severity of those symptoms.
choice_c: The medical community does not fully understand how dust-mite allergens cause allergy.
choice_d: For dust-mite allergy sufferers to get relief from their allergies, dust-mite allergens must be reduced by 90 to 95 percent.
choice_e: All of the participants in the study were told that one group in the study would be sleeping on mite-proof bedding.

stem: Five years ago, the hair dryer produced by the Wilson Appliance Company accounted for 50 percent of all sales of hair dryers nationwide. Currently, however, Wilson Appliance’s product makes up only 25 percent of such sales. Because of this decline, and because the average net income that Wilson receives per hair dryer sold has not changed over the last 5 years, the company’s net income from sales of the product must be only half of what it was 5 years ago. The reasoning in the argument is flawed because the argument
choice_a: mistakes a decline in the market share of Wilson Appliance’s hair dryer for a decline in the total sales of that product
choice_b: does not provide specific information about the profits hair dryers generate for the companies that produce them
choice_c: fails to discuss sales figures for Wilson Appliance’s products other than its hair dryers
choice_d: overlooks the possibility that the retail price of Wilson Appliance’s hair dryer may have increased over the past 5 years
choice_e: provides no independent evidence that Wilson Appliance’s hair dryer is one of the company’s least profitable products

stem: Whether or not one can rightfully call a person’s faithfulness a virtue depends in part on the object of that person’s faithfulness. Virtues are by definition praiseworthy, which is why no one considers resentment virtuous, even though it is in fact a kind of faithfulness— faithfulness to hatreds or animosities. Which one of the following most accurately expresses the overall conclusion drawn in the argument?
choice_a: The object of a person’s faithfulness partially determines whether or not that faithfulness is virtuous.
choice_b: Virtuous behavior is praiseworthy by definition.
choice_c: Behavior that emerges from hatred or animosity cannot be called virtuous.
choice_d: Faithfulness and resentment are obviously different, despite some similarities.
choice_e: Resentment should not be considered a virtuous emotion.

stem: Columnist: A government-owned water utility has received approval to collect an additional charge on water bills and to use that additional revenue to build a dam. A member of the legislature has proposed not building the dam but instead spending the extra money from water bills to build new roads. That proposal is unacceptable. Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the columnist’s judgment that the legislator’s proposal is unacceptable?
choice_a: Customers of a utility have a right to know how the money they pay to the utility will be used.
choice_b: Money designated for projects that benefit an entire community should not be used for projects that benefit only some members of a community.
